title: Models for Embryonic Respiration
author_string: Seymour, Roger S, White, Craig R
abstract:
Abstract
               Modeling has been an important tool in physiology throughout its history. In respiration physiology, for example, almost a century ago, August Krogh modeled diffusion of oxygen from a capillary (Krogh 1919b). More recently, the models of Peter Scheid and Johannes Piiper set paradigms for comparative respiration in vertebrates (Scheid 1987). The present contribution attempts to demonstrate some of the roles that modeling plays in comparative developmental physiology, particularly in embryonic respiration. It discusses the types of models, presents the hierarchical levels of modeling, evaluates the applicability and limitations of each, and rationalizes different approaches of previous studies. It also breaks new ground by considering the dependence of embryonic respiration on oxygen partial pressure and by applying finite element analysis to complex geometries of oxygen delivery.
